meta.title: E81 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW 1 Series E81 engine — N45, N46, N43, N52, M47 and N47. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the N47 timing chain risk explained. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 1 Series E81 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned E81 engine typically costs £1,500–£5,000 supply only [BMW-QUOTE], depending on engine — the N45 1.6-litre petrol sits at £1,500–£2,800, while the N52 3.0-litre straight-six can reach £2,500–£5,000. Fitting adds £520–£910. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which E81 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N52B30 3.0-litre straight-six (130i) — a naturally aspirated, proven engine from the E90-era BMWs — is widely regarded as the most reliable E81 engine. It delivers 258 bhp, superb refinement, and has a proven 150,000-mile reputation when properly maintained. The N46B20 2.0-litre petrol is also a solid, simple four-cylinder choice. Both significantly outlast the N47 diesel in terms of trouble-free mileage.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 1 Series E81?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain stretch on the N47 diesel (rear-mounted chain, cold-start rattle is the warning), injector and NOx sensor failure on the N43 petrol engines (rough running, warning lights), water pump failure on the N52 straight-six (overheating risk), and — unrelated to the engine — rear boot wiring loom fractures and rear subframe mount corrosion on all E81s.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW 1 Series E81 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends heavily on the engine and maintenance history. The N52 3.0-litre straight-six is the most reliable — it has proven durability and is well-supported by the BMW specialist community. The N46 2.0-litre petrol is also solid. The N47 diesel is a good engine but the timing chain is its defining weakness — a documented chain replacement is essential. The N43 petrols are reasonably reliable but injector issues are common. A well-maintained E81 with the right engine can be a reliable compact hatchback.

mainEntity.name: Which E81 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but the N47 diesel without documented timing chain history is the one purchase we consistently advise against. A neglected N43 petrol without injector service history is also a watch-item. The N52 straight-six and N46 four-cylinder petrols are the safest choices for both daily driving and long-term ownership.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my E81 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Key warning signs: cold-start rattle (N47 timing chain), rough running or hesitation (N43 injector/NOx sensor issues), overheating or coolant loss (N52 water pump), or loss of power and poor starting (any engine). Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an E81 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well-kept £7,000 E81, a £2,500–£4,500 N47 recon is 35–65% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £3,500 example, the same recon is 70–130% — walk away. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: if the lowest viable replacement cost exceeds 70% of the vehicle's current market value, evaluate scrap value first.

mainEntity.name: How long do E81 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N52 straight-six routinely exceeds 150,000–200,000 miles with proper servicing (regular oil changes, cooling system maintenance). The N46 and N43 four-cylinder petrols can also reach high mileages with diligent maintenance. The N47 diesel can be long-lived but the timing chain is the limiting factor — addressed proactively, it can comfortably reach 150,000+ miles. The M47 diesel is similarly durable without the chain risk.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the E81, E87, E82 and E88?

acceptedAnswer.text: All four share the first-generation 1 Series platform and most engines. The E81 is the 3-door hatchback (2007–2012, this page), the E87 is the 5-door hatchback (2004–2012), the E82 is the coupé (2007–2013), and the E88 is the convertible (2008–2013). Costs vary slightly by body style due to labour access — see each generation's own page for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW E81 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine number is stamped on the block — on four-cylinder engines (N45, N46, N43, M47, N47) it's on the left-hand side; on the N52 straight-six it's on the front of the block near the timing cover. Tell us your variant and approximate registration date and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted BMW specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: BMW 1 Series E81 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the three‑door E81 — from the N45 and N43 four‑cylinder petrols to the N52 straight‑six and the N47 diesel range. Real UK costs and honest reliability data for BMW's compact rear‑wheel‑drive hatchback. Backed by real UK enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: BMW 1 Series E81 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the three‑door E81 specifically — from the 2007 launch to the 2012 end of production.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: All ratings are [PROVISIONAL] — no dedicated Engine Page exists for any E81 engine family. Ratings are derived from triangulated specialist sources (BMW forums, PistonHeads, owner clubs). The N52B30 straight‑six is widely regarded as the most reliable of the E81 line‑up — a naturally aspirated, proven engine with a strong reputation. The N47 diesel carries the highest risk due to the rear‑mounted timing chain issue. The N43 petrols are reasonably solid but can suffer from injector and NOx sensor issues.

overview.intro: The E81 is the three‑door hatchback variant of BMW's first‑generation 1 Series, introduced in July 2007 as a sportier, more compact alternative to the five‑door E87. Built on BMW's rear‑wheel‑drive L2 platform — a layout unique among compact hatchbacks — the E81 offered engaging driving dynamics that set it apart from front‑wheel‑drive rivals. Under the bonnet, buyers could choose from a range of four‑cylinder petrol and diesel engines, plus the magnificent naturally aspirated 3.0‑litre N52 straight‑six in the 130i. Production ran until 2012, when the second‑generation F20 replaced it.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW L2 (rear‑wheel drive) • Predecessor: None (first‑generation 1 Series) • Successor: BMW F20 • Sibling body styles in this generation: E87 (5‑door hatchback), E82 (coupé), E88 (convertible).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The E81 generates consistent UK enquiries [PATTERN DATA], concentrated on 118d and 120d diesel variants as these compact hatchbacks enter affordable‑maintenance territory.

items.quote: Most dependable: The N52B30 3.0‑litre straight‑six (130i) — a naturally aspirated, proven engine from the E90‑era BMWs — offers 258 bhp, superb refinement, and a genuine 150,000‑mile reputation when properly maintained. The N43B20 2.0‑litre petrol is a solid four‑cylinder choice for daily driving. The N47 diesel carries the highest risk — the rear‑mounted timing chain is the defining weakness of this generation, with cold‑start rattle as the classic warning. Buyers should prioritise a well‑maintained N52 or N43 with documented service history, and any N47 should have proof of timing chain work.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Underpowered — fine for city use, lacks punch
Simple & Reliable — port injection, no HPFP
Best Petrol — the E81's most complete engine
Solid Diesel — pre‑N47, no timing chain risk
Risk vs Reward — budget for chain history or the job itself

economicsRule.title: The E81 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,500–£4,500 N47 recon against a £2,500–£8,000 E81 sits at 30–180% of the car's value. On a sub‑£4,000 E81, that brushes our 70% BMW threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well‑kept £7,000+ example, the same recon drops to a routine 35–65% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first.

problems.title: N47 Timing Chain Stretch
problems.rootCause: The rear‑mounted timing chain receives the last oil feed and is tensioned by a plastic guide that degrades over time — cold‑start rattle for 1–2 seconds is the classic early warning.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of cold‑start rattle, get the chain inspected immediately — a £650–£1,800 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £3,200–£5,500 replacement that follows if ignored. On an E81 worth under £4,000, this decision alone determines whether the car stays economically viable.

cta.label: Compare N47D20 engine prices for your E81 →

problems.title: N43 Petrol Injector & NOx Sensor Failure
problems.rootCause: The N43 direct‑injection petrol engines suffer from injector failure and NOx sensor degradation — leading to rough running, engine warning lights, and increased fuel consumption.
problems.recommendation: If your N43‑powered E81 suffers from rough idle or hesitation, have the injectors and NOx sensor inspected — a £200–£500 repair is far cheaper than the £2,000–£3,800 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare N43 engine prices for your E81 →

problems.title: N52 Water Pump Failure
problems.rootCause: The N52's electric water pump is a known weak point — failure leads to overheating, loss of cooling, and potential engine damage if not addressed promptly.
problems.recommendation: On any 130i, budget for water pump replacement at 80k miles — a £300–£600 preventative job is cheap insurance against the £2,500–£5,000 replacement that follows a catastrophic overheat.

cta.label: Compare N52 engine prices for your E81 →

problems.title: E81 Rear Boot Wiring Loom Failure (Non‑Engine)
problems.affectedModels: All E81 variants, particularly early 2007–2009 models.
problems.rootCause: Not an engine failure, but the most common non‑engine issue on the E81 — the wiring loom in the tailgate hinge area can fracture, causing rear light failure, central locking issues, and boot release problems.
problems.recommendation: Have the tailgate wiring inspected on any E81 — it's a common age‑related failure that can cause MOT issues if left unaddressed.

problems.title: E81 Rear Subframe Mount Corrosion (Non‑Engine)
problems.affectedModels: All E81 variants, particularly UK‑market cars.
problems.rootCause: Not an engine failure, but a known structural issue — the rear subframe mounting points can corrode, particularly in cars exposed to UK road salt.
problems.recommendation: Have the rear subframe mounts inspected on any E81 over 8 years old — it's a common MOT failure point on UK‑market cars.

replacementCosts.h2: E81 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the Model Page's baseline figures — E81 shares identical engine‑bay access with its E87 sibling, so costs are inherited directly per the Authority Model, with no divergence note required. All costs are [BMW-QUOTE] from Table A — Engine Replacement Economics.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are BMW 1 Series E81 (three‑door hatchback) only. E87 (five‑door hatchback), E82 (coupé) and E88 (convertible) are covered on their own Generation Pages, since fitment and labour access vary slightly by body style.
